Empires & Puzzles Garten Review: The Party Gargoyle Guide

Garten started as a brutal counterattacker but morphed into a sturdy healer. Is the 'Softskin' mechanic enough to keep him relevant in 2026? We break down his controversial rework, his stats compared to modern heroes, and where he still shines.
Garten Review: The Gargoyle Who Traded His Club for a First Aid Kit
Garten, The Party Gargoyle: From Retaliation to Restoration
Garten is a 5-star Nature (Green) hero from the Gargoyle family, originally released in early 2024. His journey has been... turbulent. He holds a unique place in

history, not just for his skills, but for undergoing one of the most drastic post-release reworks the game has ever seen. Initially a devastating counterattacker, he was transformed into a sturdy support healer.
state as a Healer and Softskin provider. While he may look like a fun-loving party monster ready to smash things, nowadays he's more like the designated driver ensuring everyone gets home safe.

Garten’s design is a riot of color and whimsy that stands out starkly against the usually gothic and stony aesthetic of the Gargoyle family. He looks like he crashed a medieval funeral wearing a clown suit. With his polka-dot skin, festive collar, and "Bone Club" that looks suspiciously like a maraca, he brings a much-needed splash of absurdity. It’s hard to be intimidated by a guy who looks like he’s about to twist a balloon animal, but maybe that’s his psychological warfare.

Garten's stats reveal his age. With a base Power of 1048, he sits significantly behind the modern curve. Heroes released in late 2025 and 2026 are pushing 1260+ Power, with defensive stats often exceeding 1300 unblemished.
Very low by modern standards, though less critical for a healer.
These were tank-worthy numbers in 2024, but today they are merely "decent." He relies heavily on his
passive and Limit Breaks to survive against modern snipers who can hit for 1500+ damage.
The "Bone Club Party" is a defensive toolkit packed into one button.
A solid chunk of overheal. It's a fixed number, which means it doesn't scale with troops or stats, but 700 is enough to reverse the damage of a standard slash attack or a weaker AOE.

This is his offensive edge. Reducing the enemy's max HP is permanent (mostly) and devastating against teams that rely on boosting their own health. It effectively lowers the "kill threshold" for your snipers.
This is the most complex part of his kit. All allies get
of Softskin. This reduces the next instance of damage by 90% and blocks ailments.
One charge is fragile. A single minion tap, a stray tile, or a passive damage tick will consume it instantly.
A subtle but powerful counter to heroes who block healing or inflict "toxic" healing effects.
Average. He needs 10 tiles to charge. With a Level 23 Mana troop (or Level 11 Magic/Styx), you can drop this to 9 tiles, which is the sweet spot for a healer.

By healing your team for 700 and reducing the enemy cap by 700, he creates a 1400 HP differential between the teams. In a long fight, firing him twice can reduce enemies to a range where a single sniper shot kills them.
90% reduction on a 1500 damage sniper hit saves 1350 HP. However, 90% reduction on a 50 damage minion hit saves... 45 HP. Timing is everything.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Gargoyle family bonus provides immunity to Poison damage. This is situational but helpful against specific DoT heroes.
This is what keeps Garten alive. Unlike his allies who get only 1 charge, Garten gets
every time he fires. This makes him incredibly tanky. He can often ignore the first few hits of a turn, ensuring he survives to fire again.
A fitting power for a tank/healer. It stacks with his natural bulk to keep him standing.

Upon release, Garten was a feared counterattacker. His sudden change to a healer caused an uproar, but it arguably gave him more longevity than a raw damage dealer, as damage dealers fade faster due to power creep.
In the current high-power meta, Garten is a
. He is not the top dog anymore.
His stats make him vulnerable without heavy investment (LB2).
His speed (Average) is slow compared to the "Very Fast" rush of modern battles.
remains a potent mechanic that doesn't age—700 HP off the top is always relevant.
Maximizes his survivability. His job is to cast Softskin and Heal; his attack stat is irrelevant for these roles.
His tile damage will be negligible.
Prioritizes raw HP, which synergizes with his overheal potential.
Lower defense might make him take more damage per hit once Softskin wears off.
path is the way to go. You want him to survive long enough to fire his skill multiple times.
The self-Softskin (4 charges) makes him very hard to kill with special skills alone.
Reduces enemy Max HP, countering the popular overheal meta.
The ally Softskin can completely neuter a massive enemy sniper hit if timed perfectly.
Immunity to healing ailments protects your team's recovery engine.
The 1-charge Softskin on allies is too easily stripped by minions or tile damage.
700 HP is good, but percentage-based healers scale better with the massive HP pools of modern heroes (2500+).
He doesn't help you kill quickly; he helps you survive slowly. In timed modes, this can be a liability.
Can be too slow to save a team against a bad board.
At Very Fast speed, Garten becomes a nightmare. He can keep Softskin up almost permanently.
His overheal and damage reduction make hard bosses trivial.
Still annoying to kill, though less threatening than before.
Garten (Tank) flanked by fast dodgers or other damage mitigators (e.g., Constance, C. Kadilen).
Layering defenses forces the enemy to bring a dispeller or specific counters, slowing them down.
Garten + Another Max HP Reducer (e.g., Furdinand, bubbles).
Stack the max HP reduction. Two casts from Garten and one from a partner can drop the enemy team to 30% max HP, making them one-shot kills for anyone.
Minions are Garten's worst nightmare. Their petty little hits strip the Softskin charges instantly, wasting the 90% reduction.
While Softskin is undispellable, his healing immunity buff is not.
Can often kill a teammate before Garten charges up.
If you pulled Garten, you have a solid anchor for your team. He will keep your weaker heroes alive through tough map stages and quests. Focus on keeping him alive; don't worry about his damage.
Garten is a niche tool for your depth teams. He shines in

or against specific passive teams where you have time to whittle down their max HP. Do not prioritize Alpha Aethers for him unless you lack modern green healers; save those for heroes with current-gen stats.

When released, Garten was a counterattacker. Players spent resources leveling him for that role. Then, in the May 2024 "Balance Update," SG removed the counterattack and replaced it with the heal/softskin kit.
To say players were "upset" is an understatement. Phrases like "complete fraud," "robbery," and demands for "Alpha Reset Tokens" flooded the forums. One user noted,
"I feel cheated... it completely turns this character into something completely different."
While the dust has settled, many veterans still view Garten with a mix of suspicion and regret, not because he is bad, but because he isn't what they bought.

Garten is the hero who showed up to the party with a bat and left with a stethoscope. He is a capable, durable healer who offers unique protection mechanics. While he lacks the raw stats of the 2026 meta, his
ensure he still has a seat at the table—even if it's at the kids' table in top-tier wars.
A solid, if controversial, defensive pivot. Great for Rush, decent for support, but forever haunted by the ghost of the counterattacker he used to be.
A comprehensive review of Garten from the Gargoyle family in Empires & Puzzles. Analysis of his healing rework, Softskin mechanics, and modern viability.
